In the case of the absence of a diacritic in Syriac or CPA, this demonstrative can also be distinguished from the independent pronoun "she" when it is used as the object of a preposition.

entries.form_section: In Jewish texts (BA, Qum, and LJLA in particular), the 3ms. sg. is usually lhwh to avoid homography w. the Tetragrammaton. The imperf. is often apocopated to y/nhē in most dialects, especially as a jussive, possibly for similar reasons in at least some cases.
entries.end_notes:
As a marker of tense in Syriac, the initial heh is almost always elided.
The sole example of Dt, from BarBahlul cited by LS, is almost certainly to be understood as a misvocalized form of the relatively well-attested Ct; see Payne-Smith Thesaurus col. 986.
See also note s.v. lˀw adv.

The use of the encliticized perfect as a tense modifier in Syriac is extremely complex and varies not only from author to author but even within a single text in the individual dialects of the speakers compared to each other and to the narration! It is probable that in at least some of the earliest texts the imperfectivity was still felt, although not so later on, but the final word has yet to be said on the subject.
